title: Kubernetes 101 — Fundamentals
description: Validate your understanding of Kubernetes and Dynatrace application monitoring as covered in this lab: pods, namespaces, kubectl, the Dynatrace Operator, and OneAgent injection.
imagine: You just finished the Kubernetes 101 enablement. The TODO app is running on a k3d cluster, instrumented by Dynatrace OneAgent in AppOnly mode. You want to confirm you understood how everything fits together.
yourGoal: Answer six questions about Kubernetes fundamentals and Dynatrace application monitoring as they applied to this lab.

story.introduction: The lab deployed a k3d Kubernetes cluster with a TODO app and the Dynatrace Operator in AppOnly mode. These questions reinforce what each component does.
story.context: Everything you need to answer can be found in the lab pages you just completed.

questions.title: What is a Kubernetes namespace?
questions.content: The lab used two namespaces: `dynatrace` and `todoapp`. What is the main purpose of namespaces in Kubernetes?

options.text: A logical partition that groups related resources and allows separate access control and resource quotas
options.explanation: Correct! Namespaces let you isolate teams, applications, or environments within the same cluster.

options.text: A physical network boundary that prevents pods from talking to each other
options.explanation: Namespaces are logical, not physical. Pods across namespaces can still communicate unless blocked by NetworkPolicies.

options.text: A separate Kubernetes cluster running inside the main cluster
options.explanation: Namespaces share the same cluster control plane — they are not sub-clusters.

options.text: A Docker image registry used by pods to pull container images
options.explanation: Image registries are external. Namespaces are resource-grouping constructs within the cluster.

questions.explanation: Kubernetes namespaces partition cluster resources into isolated groups. In this lab, all Dynatrace components lived in `dynatrace` and the TODO app lived in `todoapp`.
hints: Think about why `kubectl get pods -n dynatrace` and `kubectl get pods -n todoapp` return different results.
The key word is 'logical' — namespaces do not map to physical nodes.

questions.title: Listing pods in a namespace
questions.content: Which kubectl command lists all pods running in the `todoapp` namespace?

options.text: kubectl get pods -n todoapp
options.explanation: Correct! The -n flag selects the namespace.

options.text: kubectl list pods --namespace=todoapp
options.explanation: 'list' is not a kubectl verb — use 'get'.

options.text: kubectl describe pods todoapp
options.explanation: describe shows details of a named resource, not a pod list across a namespace.

options.text: kubectl pods --in todoapp
options.explanation: Not valid kubectl syntax.

questions.explanation: kubectl uses verb-noun syntax. 'get pods' lists pods; '-n <namespace>' scopes the query to that namespace.

questions.title: Why do pods need a restart after deploying OneAgent?
questions.content: In Section 3 you ran `kubectl rollout restart deployment -n todoapp`. Why is this restart necessary for Dynatrace instrumentation?

options.text: The Dynatrace Operator needs a reboot to activate its licence
options.explanation: Dynatrace licensing is not tied to pod restarts.

options.text: OneAgent's mutating webhook only injects the agent into pods at their creation time, so existing pods must be restarted to pick up the injection
options.explanation: Correct! The webhook intercepts new pods during scheduling. Pods that were already running before the DynaKube was applied never passed through the webhook.

options.text: Kubernetes requires a full cluster restart whenever a new CRD is installed
options.explanation: CRDs extend the API without requiring cluster or pod restarts.

options.text: The TODO app needs to re-read the DT_ENVIRONMENT variable from a ConfigMap
options.explanation: Environment variables alone don't instrument an application — the agent library must be injected into the process.

questions.explanation: Dynatrace uses a Kubernetes mutating admission webhook. Webhooks run at pod creation time, so any pods that were already running when the DynaKube was applied need to be recycled before the agent is injected.
hints: Think about when Kubernetes calls a mutating webhook.
The annotation `oneagent.dynatrace.com/injected: true` is only present on pods that went through the webhook.

questions.title: What does the ActiveGate do?
questions.content: After applying the DynaKube, an ActiveGate pod started in the `dynatrace` namespace. What is the role of the ActiveGate?

options.text: It is the Kubernetes API server proxy that the operator uses to watch for CRD changes
options.explanation: The operator connects to the Kubernetes API server directly — the ActiveGate is not involved in that path.

options.text: It acts as a secure communication gateway that routes monitoring data from OneAgent to your Dynatrace tenant
options.explanation: Correct! The ActiveGate is a local relay. OneAgent sends data to the ActiveGate, which forwards it to the Dynatrace SaaS backend — reducing direct outbound connections from each node.

options.text: It stores all collected metrics locally so the cluster works without internet access
options.explanation: The ActiveGate forwards data to the tenant; it does not provide offline storage.

options.text: It runs a Prometheus scrape loop that collects metrics from every pod
options.explanation: The ActiveGate proxies Dynatrace protocol traffic, not Prometheus scrapes.

questions.explanation: The ActiveGate is a local routing proxy. OneAgent on each instrumented pod communicates with the local ActiveGate, which then securely forwards all observability data to the Dynatrace SaaS tenant.
hints: Think about the data path: pod → ? → Dynatrace cloud.
The ActiveGate sits between OneAgent and the Dynatrace backend.

questions.title: What is the DynaKube custom resource?
questions.content: You applied a `dynakube.yaml` file in Section 2. What does the DynaKube CR tell the Dynatrace Operator?

options.text: It is a Helm values file that overrides chart defaults
options.explanation: Helm values are separate from DynaKube CRs. The DynaKube is a Kubernetes Custom Resource, not a Helm file.

options.text: It is a Kubernetes secret that stores the DT_OPERATOR_TOKEN
options.explanation: Token secrets are a separate Kubernetes Secret object. The DynaKube CR defines the desired monitoring configuration.

options.text: It defines the desired monitoring configuration: which tenant to connect to, which components to deploy (ActiveGate, CSI driver), and which instrumentation mode to use
options.explanation: Correct! The DynaKube CR is the single source of truth for how Dynatrace should monitor your cluster. The operator continuously reconciles the cluster state to match what the DynaKube defines.

options.text: It is a NetworkPolicy that allows traffic from OneAgent to the internet
options.explanation: NetworkPolicies are a standard Kubernetes resource. The DynaKube is a Dynatrace-specific CRD for monitoring configuration.

questions.explanation: The DynaKube Custom Resource is the control plane for Dynatrace observability. It specifies tenant credentials reference, monitoring mode (AppOnly in this lab), and which components to provision. The operator watches DynaKube objects and reconciles the cluster to match.
hints: Think of the DynaKube like a deployment spec — it describes the desired state, and the operator makes it happen.
It references the secret that holds your DT_OPERATOR_TOKEN.

questions.title: How do you confirm OneAgent was injected into a pod?
questions.content: After restarting the todoapp pods, what is the most direct way to confirm that OneAgent was successfully injected?

options.text: Check that the pod has the annotation `oneagent.dynatrace.com/injected: "true"` set by the mutating webhook
options.explanation: Correct! The mutating webhook stamps this annotation on every pod it successfully injects. Running `kubectl get pods -n todoapp -o yaml` and looking for this annotation is the definitive local check.

options.text: Look for a new container named `oneagent` inside the pod
options.explanation: AppOnly mode uses init containers and a shared volume to inject code modules — it does not add a running sidecar container.

options.text: Check that the pod's CPU usage doubled after the restart
options.explanation: OneAgent has minimal CPU overhead and CPU usage is not a reliable injection indicator.

options.text: The Dynatrace Operator logs will print 'injection complete' for each pod
options.explanation: The injection is handled by the mutating webhook, not the operator controller — and the evidence lives in the pod annotation.

questions.explanation: The annotation `oneagent.dynatrace.com/injected: "true"` is stamped by the Dynatrace mutating webhook at pod admission time. It is the canonical proof that the agent was injected. In this lab the shell validation used `kubectl get pods -n todoapp -o jsonpath=...` to check exactly this.
hints: The lab's shell validation for Section 3 checked this exact annotation.
Run `kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n todoapp` and look at the Annotations section.

learningObjectives: Explain the role of Kubernetes namespaces
Use kubectl to inspect pods in a specific namespace
Describe why a pod restart is needed after deploying OneAgent in AppOnly mode
Explain the role of the ActiveGate in the Dynatrace data path
Understand what the DynaKube custom resource defines
Identify the pod annotation that confirms OneAgent injection
